figures.caption: Figure 3: Examples of the datasets used.

Figure 1: Top: Typical archival access pipelines rely primarily on (A) OCR and (B) keywordbased search. Bottom: Our contributions: 1) we use LLMs to correct OCR errors, 2) support natural language queries over the historical archive, and 3) create new AR/VR interfaces.

Table 3: Ablation and performance comparison across retrieval depths (k ∈ {5, 10, 25}) with and without reranking. LLM(·) denotes the same retrieval configuration applied to the LLM-corrected OCR view of the corpus. Dense retrieval uses text-embedding-2-preview, and answer generation is performed using GPT-4o-mini. The ± limits denote the 95% confidence interval. Superscripts ∗ , ∗∗, and ∗∗∗ indicate statistically significant improvement over the second-best method at levels p < 0.1, p < 0.05, and p < 0.01 respectively, according to a paired t-test.
Figure 4: Example: Raw OCR (Tesseract) vs. raw OCR with LLM refinement (Gemini 3 Pro) for a page snippet.
Figure 7: Examples of two queries, the prototypical and the actual reply by the RAG system. We also show indicative documents retrieved by the search process.
Figure 6: Deployed system, reducing system response time by changing the reranker and shifting to streaming LLM text generation (candidate documents L = 50 for reranking).
Figure 8: Prototypical interface using the Vision Pro augmented reality device
Figure 5: Reranking trade-off between quality (NDCG@10) and mean time (candidate documents L = 50 used for reranking).
